title: DEEP NETWORKS FROM THE PRINCIPLE OF RATE REDUCTION [PDF]

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## DEEP NETWORKS FROM THE PRINCIPLE OF RATE REDUCTION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

This work attempts to interpret modern deep (convolutional) networks from the principles of rate reduction and (shift) invariant classification. We show that the basic iterative gradient ascent scheme for maximizing the rate reduction of learned features naturally leads to a deep network, one iteration per layer. The architectures, operators (linear or nonlinear), and parameters of the network are all explicitly constructed layer-by-layer in a forward propagation fashion. All components of this “white box” network have precise optimization, statistical, and geometric interpretation. Our preliminary experiments indicate that such a network can already learn a good discriminative deep representation without any back propagation training. Moreover, all linear operators of the so-derived network naturally become multi-channel convolutions when we enforce classification to be rigorously shift-invariant. The derivation also indicates that such a convolutional network is significantly more efficient to learn and construct in the spectral domain.

## 1 INTRODUCTION AND MOTIVATION

In recent years, various deep (convolution) network architectures such as AlexNet (Krizhevsky et al., 2012), VGG (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2015), ResNet (He et al., 2016), DenseNet (Huang et al., 2017), Recurrent CNN, LSTM (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997), Capsule Networks (Hinton et al., 2011), etc., have demonstrated very good performance in classification tasks of real-world datasets such as speeches or images. Nevertheless, almost all such networks are developed through years of empirical _trial and error_ , including both their architectures/operators and the ways they are to be effectively trained. Some recent practices even take to the extreme by searching for effective network structures and training strategies through extensive random search techniques, such as Neural Architecture Search (Zoph & Le, 2017; Baker et al., 2017), AutoML (Hutter et al., 2019), and Learning to Learn (Andrychowicz et al., 2016).

Despite tremendous empirical advances, there is still a lack of rigorous theoretical justification of the need or reasons for “deep” network architectures and a lack of fundamental understanding of the associated operators (e.g. multi-channel convolution and nonlinear activation) in each layer. As a result, deep networks are often designed and trained heuristically and then used as a “black box.” There have been a severe lack of guiding principles for each of the stages: For a given task, how wide or deep the network should be? What are the roles and relationships among the multiple (convolution) channels? Which parts of the networks need to be learned and trained and which can be determined in advance? How to evaluate the optimality of the resulting network? As a consequence, besides empirical evaluation, it is usually impossible to offer any rigorous guarantees for certain performance of a trained network, such as invariance to transformation (Azulay & Weiss, 2018; Engstrom et al., 2017) or overfitting noisy or even arbitrary labels (Zhang et al., 2017).

In this paper, we do not intend to address all these questions but we would attempt to offer a plausible interpretation of deep (convolution) neural networks by deriving a class of deep networks _from first principles_ . We contend that all key features and structures of modern deep (convolution) neural networks can be naturally derived from optimizing a principled objective, namely the _rate reduction_ recently proposed by Yu et al. (2020), that seeks a compact discriminative (invariant) representation of the data. More specifically, the basic iterative _gradient ascent_ scheme for optimizing the objective naturally takes the form of a deep neural network, one layer per iteration.

This principled approach brings a couple of nice surprises: First, architectures, operators, and parameters of the network can be constructed explicitly layer-by-layer in a _forward propagation_ fashion, and all inherit precise optimization, statistical and geometric interpretation. As result, the so constructed “white box” deep network already gives a good discriminative representation (and achieves good classification performance) _without any back propagation_ for training the deep network. Second, in the case of seeking a representation _rigorously_ invariant to shift or translation, the network naturally lends itself to a multi-channel convolutional network. Moreover, the derivation indicates such a convolutional network is computationally more efficient to learn and construct in the _spectral_

2 TECHNICAL APPROACH

Consider a basic classification task: given a set of _m_ samples _**X**_ = _[.]_ [ _**x**_[1] _, . . . ,_ _**x**[m]_ ] _∈_ R _[n][×][m]_ and their associated memberships _**π**_ ( _**x**[i]_ ) _∈_ [ _k_ ] in _k_ different classes, a deep network is typically used to model a direct mapping from the input data _**x** ∈_ R _[n]_ to its class label _f_ ( _**x** ,_ _**θ**_ ) : _**x** �→_ _**y** ∈_ R _[k]_ , where _**y**_ is typically a “one-hot” vector encoding the membership information _**π**_ ( _**x**_ ): the _j_ -th entry of _**y**_ is 1 iff _**π**_ ( _**x**_ ) = _j_ . The parameters _**θ**_ of the network is typically learned to minimize certain prediction loss, say the cross entropy loss, via gradient-descent type back propagation. Although this popular approach provides people a direct and effective way to train a network that predicts the class information, the so learned representation is however implicit and lacks clear interpretation.

## 2.1 PRINCIPLE OF RATE REDUCTION AND GROUP INVARIANCE

**The Principle of Maximal Coding Rate Reduction.** To help better understand features learned in a deep network, the recent work of Yu et al. (2020) has argued that the goal of (deep) learning is to learn a compact discriminative and diverse feature representation[1] _**z**_ = _f_ ( _**x**_ ) _∈_ R _[n]_ of the data _**x** f_ ( _**x**_ ) _h_ ( _**z**_ ) before any subsequent tasks such as classification: _**x** −−−→_ _**z** −−−→_ _**y** ._ To be more precise, instead of directly fitting the class label _**y**_ , a principled objective is to learn a feature map _f_ ( _**x**_ ) : _**x** �→_ _**z**_ which transforms the data _**x**_ onto a set of most discriminative low-dimensional linear subspaces _{S[j] }[k] j_ =1 _[⊂]_[R] _[n]_[, one subspace] _[ S][j]_[per class] _[ j][∈]_[[] _[k]_[]][.]

Let _**Z**_ = _[.]_ [ _**z**_[1] _, . . . ,_ _**z**[m]_ ] = [ _f_ ( _**x**_[1] ) _, . . . , f_ ( _**x**[m]_ )] be the features of the given samples _**X**_ . WLOG, we may assume all features _**z**[i]_ are normalized to be of unit norm: _**z**[i] ∈_ S _[n][−]_[1] . For convenience, let **Π** _[j] ∈_ R _[m][×][m]_ be a diagonal matrix whose diagonal entries encode the membership of samples/features belong to the _j_ -th class: **Π** _[j]_ ( _i, i_ ) = _**π**_ ( _**x**[i]_ ) = _**π**_ ( _**z**[i]_ ). Then based on principles from lossy data compression (Ma et al., 2007), Yu et al. (2020) has suggested that the optimal representation _**Z** ⋆ ⊂_ S _[n][−]_[1] should maximize the following coding rate reduction objective, known as the MCR[2] principle:

where _α_ = _n/_ ( _mϵ_[2] ), _αj_ = _n/_ (tr( **Π** _[j]_ ) _ϵ_[2] ), _γj_ = tr( **Π** _[j]_ ) _/m_ for _j_ = 1 _, . . . , k_ . Given a prescribed quantization error _ϵ_ , the first term _R_ of ∆ _R_ ( _**Z**_ ) measures the total coding length for all the features _**Z**_ and the second term _Rc_ is the sum of coding lengths for features in each of the _k_ classes.

In Yu et al. (2020), the authors have shown the optimal representation _**Z** ⋆_ that maximizes the above object indeed has desirable nice properties. Nevertheless, they adopted a conventional deep network (e.g. the ResNet) as a black box to model and parameterize the feature mapping: _**z**_ = _f_ ( _**x** ,_ _**θ**_ ). It has empirically shown that with such a choice, one can effectively optimize the MCR[2] objective and obtain discriminative and diverse representations for classifying real image data sets.

However, there remain several unanswered problems. Although the resulting feature representation is more interpretable, the network itself is still not. It is _not_ clear why any chosen network is able to optimize the desired MCR[2] objective: Would there be any potential limitations? The good empirical results (say with a ResNet) do not necessarily justify the particular choice in architectures and operators of the network: Why is a layered model necessary, how wide and deep is adequate, and is there any rigorous justification for the use of convolutions and nonlinear operators used? In Section 2.2, we show that using gradient ascent to maximize the rate reduction ∆ _R_ ( _**Z**_ ) naturally leads to a “white box” deep network that represents such a mapping. All linear/nonlinear operators and parameters of the network are _explicitly constructed in a purely forward propagation fashion_ . **Group Invariant Rate Reduction.** So far, we have considered the data and features as vectors. In many applications, such as serial data or imagery data, the semantic meaning (labels) of the data and their features are _invariant_ to certain transformations g _∈_ G (for some group G) (Cohen & Welling, 2016). For example, the meaning of an audio signal is invariant to shift in time; and the identity of an object in an image is invariant to translation in the image plane. Hence, we prefer the feature mapping _f_ ( _**x** ,_ _**θ**_ ) is rigorously invariant to such transformations:

The recent work of Zaheer et al. (2017); Maron et al. (2020) characterize properties of networks and operators for set permutation groups. Nevertheless, it remains challenging to learn features via a deep network that are _guaranteed_ to be invariant even to simple transformations such as translation and rotation (Azulay & Weiss, 2018; Engstrom et al., 2017). In Section 2.3, we show that the MCR[2] principle is compatible with invariance in a very natural and precise way: we only need to assign all transformed versions _{_ _**x** ◦_ g _|_ g _∈_ G _}_ into the same class as _**x**_ and map them all to the same subspace _S_ .[2] We will rigorously show (in the Appendices) that, when the group G is (discrete) circular 1D shifting or 2D translation, the resulting deep network naturally becomes a _multi-channel convolution network_ !

Notice that all parameters of the network are explicitly constructed layer by layer in a _forward propagation_ fashion. Once constructed, there is no need of any additional supervised learning, say via back propagation. As suggested in Yu et al. (2020), the so learned features can be directly used for classification via a nearest subspace classifier.

**Comparison with Other Approaches and Architectures.** Structural similarities between deep networks and iterative optimization schemes, especially those for solving sparse coding, have been long noticed. In particular, Gregor & LeCun (2010) has argued that algorithms for sparse coding, such as the FISTA algorithm (Beck & Teboulle, 2009), can be viewed as a deep network and be trained for better coding performance, known as LISTA. Later Monga et al. (2019); Sun et al. (2020) have proposed similar interpretation of deep networks as unrolling algorithms for sparse coding. Like all networks that are inspired by unfolding certain iterative optimization schemes, the structure of the ReduNet naturally contains a skip connection between adjacent layers as in the ResNet (He

But a major difference here is that all components (layers, channels, and operators) of the ReduNet are by explicit construction from first principles and they all have precise optimization, statistical and geometric interpretation. Furthermore, there is no need to learn them from back-propagation, although in principle one still could if further fine-tuning of the network is needed (see Remark 3 of Appendix A for more discussions).

## 2.3 DEEP CONVOLUTION NETWORKS FROM SHIFT-INVARIANT RATE REDUCTION

We next examine ReduNet from the perspective of invariance to transformation. Using the basic and important case of shift/translation invariance as an example, we will show that for data which are compatible with an invariant classifier, the ReduNet construction automatically takes the form of _a (multi-channel) convolutional neural network_ , rather than heuristically imposed upon.

**1D Serial Data and Shift Invariance.** For one-dimensional data _**x** ∈_ R _[n]_ under shift symmetry, we take G to be the group of circular shifts. Each observation _**x**[i]_ generates a family _{_ _**x**[i] ◦_ g _|_ g _∈_ G _}_ of shifted copies, which are the columns of the circulant matrix circ( _**x**[i]_ ) _∈_ R _[n][×][n]_ (see Appendix B.1 or Kra & Simanca (2012) for properties of circulant matrices).
What happens if we construct the ReduNet from these families _**Z**_ 1 = [circ( _**x**_[1] ) _, . . . ,_ circ( _**x**[m]_ )]? The data covariance matrix:

associated with this family of samples is _automatically_ a (symmetric) circulant matrix. Moreover, because the circulant property is preserved under sums, inverses, and products, the matrices _**E**_ 1 and _**C**_ 1 _[j]_[are][also][automatically][circulant][matrices,][whose][application][to][a][feature][vector] _**[z]**_[can][be] implemented using cyclic convolution “⊛” (see Proposition B.1 of Appendix B):

Because _g_ ( _·,_ _**θ**_ 1) consists only of operations that co-vary with cyclic shifts, the features _**Z**_ 2 at the next level again consist of families of shifts: _**Z**_ 2 = �circ( _**x**_[1] + _ηg_ ( _**x**_[1] _,_ _**θ**_ 1)) _, . . . ,_ circ( _**x**[m]_ + _ηg_ ( _**x**[m] ,_ _**θ** m_ ))� _._ Continuing inductively, we see that all matrices _**E** ℓ_ and _**C** ℓ[j]_[based on such] _**[ Z]**[ℓ]_[are circulant. By virtue] of the properties of the data, ReduNet has taken the form of a convolutional network, _with no need to explicitly choose this structure!_

**The Role of Multiple Channels and Sparsity.** There is one problem though: In general, the set of all circular permutations of a vector _**z**_ give a full-rank matrix. That is, the _n_ “augmented” features associated with each sample (hence each class) typically already span the entire space R _[n]_ . The MCR[2] objective (1) will not be able to distinguish classes as different subspaces.

One natural remedy is to improve the separability of the data by “lifting” the features to a higher dimensional space, e.g., by taking their responses to multiple, filters _**k**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**k** C ∈_ R _[n]_ :

However, this operation does not yet render the classes separable – features associated with other classes will span the _same n_ -dimensional subspace. This reflects a fundamental conflict between linear (subspace) modeling and invariance. One way of resolving this conflict is to leverage additional structure within each class, in the form of _sparsity_ : signals within each class can be assumed to be generated not as arbitrary linear combinations of basis vectors, but as sparse combinations of atoms of different (incoherent) dictionaries _Dj_ . Under this assumption, if the convolution kernels _{_ _**k** c}_ match well the sparsifying dictionaries,[7] the multi-channel responses should be sparse. Hence we may take an entry-wise _sparsity-promoting nonlinear thresholding_ , say _**τ**_ ( _·_ ), on the filter outputs by setting small (say absolute value below _ϵ_ ) or negative responses to be zero:[8]
These features can be assumed to lie on a lower-dimensional (nonlinear) submanifold of R _[n][×][C]_ , which can be linearized and separated from the other classes by subsequent ReduNet layers.

This multi-channel ReduNet retains the good invariance properties described above: all _**E** ℓ_ and _**C** ℓ[j]_ matrices are block circulant, and represent _multi-channel 1D circular convolutions_ (see Proposition B.2 of Appendix B for a rigorous statement and proof):

Hence by construction, the resulting ReduNet is a deep convolutional network for multi-channel 1D signals. Unlike Xception nets Chollet (2017), these multi-channel convolutions in general are _not_ depthwise separable.[9]

**Connections to Recurrent and Convolutional Sparse Coding.** The sparse coding perspective of Gregor & LeCun (2010) is later extended to recurrent and convolutional networks for serial data, e.g. Wisdom et al. (2016); Papyan et al. (2016); Sulam et al. (2018); Monga et al. (2019). Although both sparsity and convolution are advocated as desired characteristics for deep networks, they do not explicitly justify the necessity of sparsity and convolutions from the objective of the network, say classification. In our framework, we see how multi-channel convolutions ( _**E**_[¯] _,_ _**C**_[¯] _[j]_ ), different � nonlinear activations ( _**π**[j] ,_ _**τ**_ ), and the sparsity requirement are derived _from_ , rather than heuristically proposed for, the objective of maximizing rate reduction of the features while enforcing shift invariance.

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

We now _verify_ whether the so constructed ReduNet achieves its design objectives through experiments on synthetic data and real images. The datasets and experiments are chosen to clearly demonstrate the behaviors of the network obtained by our algorithm, in terms of learning the correct discriminative representation and truly achieving invariance. It is not the purpose of this work to push the state of the art on any real datasets with highly engineered networks and systems, although we believe this framework has this potential in the future. All code is implemented in Python mainly using NumPy. All our experiments are conducted in a computer with 2.8 GHz Intel i7 CPU and 16GB of memory. 4 CONCLUSIONS AND FUTURE WORK

This work offers an interpretation of deep (convolutional) networks by construction from first principles. It provides a rigorous explanation for the deep architecture and components from the perspective of optimizing the rate reduction objective. Simulations and experiments on basic data sets clearly verify the so-constructed ReduNet achieves the desired functionality and objective. Although in this work the ReduNet is forward constructed, one may study how to effectively fine tune it via back propagation. We believe rate reduction provides a principled framework for designing new networks with interpretable architectures and operators that can scale up to real-world datasets and problems, with better performance guarantees. This framework can also be naturally extended to settings of _online_ or _unsupervised_ learning if **Π** is partially or not known and is to be optimized.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper proposes a compelling "white box" framework for deep learning by deriving network architectures from the principle of Maximal Coding Rate Reduction ($\text{MCR}^2$). The work's greatest strength lies in its theoretical clarity, attempting to replace the "black box" heuristic of backpropagation and manual architecture search with a principled, layered construction based on gradient ascent of a geometric objective. By demonstrating that multi-channel convolutions and nonlinearities naturally emerge from the pursuit of shift-invariant representations, the authors provide a rigorous justification for components that are typically justified only through empirical success. However, the reliance on a "forward propagation" construction without backpropagation raises significant questions regarding its scalability to large-scale, complex datasets like ImageNet, where fine-tuned local features often outperform purely geometric descriptors. Additionally, while the spectral domain interpretation is elegant, the paper must address the practical computational overhead of the proposed iterative scheme compared to highly optimized standard CNN implementations. Overall, this is a high-quality, potentially seminal contribution that bridges the gap between statistical signal processing and deep learning.

comments.title: Subspace rank collapse in shift-invariant representations
comments.quote: In general, the set of all circular permutations of a vector $z$ give a full-rank matrix. That is, the $n$ “augmented” features associated with each sample (hence each class) typically already span the entire space $\mathbb{R}^n$. The $\text{MCR}^2$ objective (1) will not be able to distinguish classes as different subspaces.
comments.explanation: The authors identify that the orbit of a signal under a shift group spans $\mathbb{R}^n$, making subspace-based discrimination impossible. They propose 'lifting' to $nC$ dimensions. However, lifting via linear convolutions does not inherently increase the rank of the orbit in a way that creates separability. The $n$ shifted versions of the lifted signal still span a maximum of $n$ degrees of freedom. Without non-linearity or sparsity, the classes likely still overlap in the lifted space. The claim that lifting alone solves the 'full-rank' problem is mathematically underspecified.

comments.title: Dimensionality mismatch and unclear notation for multi-channel covariance
comments.quote: This operation lifts each original feature vector $z$ channels of a feature $\in \mathbb{R}^n$ to a $C$-channel feature... denoted $\bar{z}_{vec} ... \in \mathbb{R}^{nC}$... the associated circulant version... $\in \mathbb{R}^{C \times n}$.
comments.explanation: There is a dimensionality conflict in the 'lifting' description. A signal with $C$ channels and length $n$ under circulant shifts should be represented by a matrix in $\mathbb{R}^{nC \times n}$. A $C 	imes n$ matrix is insufficient. Additionally, for multi-channel data, the covariance $\Sigma$ is 'block-circulant', not simply circulant. This distinction is vital for the claim that linear operators 'naturally' become multi-channel convolutions, as standard convolutions correspond to simple circulant matrices.

comments.title: Potential Misidentification of Class Weights in MCR Definitions
comments.explanation: In the standard MCR2 formulation (Yu et al., 2020), $\gamma_j$ is typically the ratio of the number of samples in class $j$ to the total number of samples ($m_j / m$). The text defines $\gamma_j = \text{tr}(\Pi_j)/m$, which is consistent. However, $\alpha$ is usually a constant related to the precision $\epsilon$. In many formulations of MCR2, the term $\alpha_j$ is defined as $\frac{n}{m_j \epsilon^2}$ effectively. If the authors later derive the gradient $\frac{\partial \Delta R}{\partial \mathbf{Z}}$, a slight discrepancy in how the $\gamma_j$ weights are applied inside or outside the log-det terms can change the resulting 'network' architecture. Given that $\alpha$ also contains $1/m$, the interplay between $\alpha$ and $\alpha_j$ requires careful verification against the intended expanded log-det formula to ensure the gradient (6) truly maps to the proposed layer (8).

comments.title: Conflicting definitions of increment transform g
comments.quote: By inspecting the structure of the gradient (6), it suggests that a natural candidate for the increment transform _g_ ( _**z** ℓ,_ _**θ** ℓ_ ) is of the form: ... [Equation 8] ... _j_ =1 where _**π**[j]_ ( _**z** ℓ_ ) _∈_ [0 _,_ 1] indicates the probability of _**z** ℓ_ belonging to the _j_ -th class.
comments.explanation: Equation 8 (though the OCR of the formula itself is messy, the surrounding text defines its structure) describes $g$ as a summation over $k$ classes weighted by membership probabilities $\pi_j$. However, in the footnote 3, the authors state that on training samples where identities are known, this $g$ gives 'exactly the values for the gradient'. Looking at the key equations for the average class coding rate $R_c$, the gradient of $R_c$ with respect to $Z$ would indeed involve a sum over classes. But the total rate $R(Z, \epsilon)$ is independent of class labels. Its gradient involves a term that applies to the entire dataset (often represented by the $E_\ell$ term mentioned later in the text). If $g$ is defined strictly as a sum over $j=1 \dots k$ weighted by class probabilities, it is unclear how it captures the gradient of the first term $R(Z, \epsilon)$ unless one of the indices represents the global term or the summation logic is more nuanced. The text suggests $g$ takes the form of Equation 8, yet Equation 8 is then described as the 'second term' of a larger construction in the following paragraph. This creates ambiguity about whether $g$ is the full update or just the class-dependent part of the update.

comments.title: Logical gap in membership estimation via Euclidean projection
comments.quote: the term _**p**[j] ℓ_ = _._ _**C** ℓj_ _**[z]**[ℓ]_[can be viewed as projection of] _**[ z]**[ℓ]_[onto the orthogonal complement of each] class _j_ . Therefore, _\parallel_ _**p**[j] ℓ[\parallel]_[2][ is small if] _**[ z]**[ℓ]_[is in class] _[ j]_[ and large otherwise.
comments.explanation: The authors justify using a softmax on the norm of the projection onto the orthogonal complement to estimate class membership. While this is geometrically intuitive for refined subspaces, it creates a 'chicken-and-egg' problem for a forward-only high-level construction. In early layers ($\ell=0$ or $1$), the features $z$ are not yet mapped to discriminative subspaces; they are essentially the raw data $x$. If the features are not yet separated, the projection onto the (initially calculated) orthogonal complements may not provide a meaningful signal for the softmax to 'route' the gradient correctly. The paper claims the network works 'without any back propagation', which implies these weights $C_j$ are calculated from the current layer's statistics. If the current layer's statistics are not yet class-discriminative, the membership estimation $\pi(z)$ will be noisy or uniform, potentially stalling the gradient ascent. This is a significant conceptual hurdle for a 'forward-only' white-box construction compared to learned networks.

comments.title: Subspace rank limitation in shift-invariant lifting
comments.quote: the set of all circular permutations of a vector _**z**_ give a full-rank matrix. That is, the _n_ “augmented” features associated with each sample (hence each class) typically already span the entire space R _[n]_ . The MCR[2] objective (1) will not be able to distinguish classes as different subspaces.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that circulant augmentations of a single vector usually span $\mathbb{R}^n$, making subspace separation impossible. They propose 'lifting' to $C$ channels. However, they then state: 'Then the columns of circ(¯ _**z**_ ) will only span at most an _n_ -dimension'. If the lifted space is $\mathbb{R}^{nC}$ and the subspace spanned by the shifts is still $n$-dimensional (which it is, as there are only $n$ possible shifts), the relative 'low-dimensionality' is $n / (nC) = 1/C$. While this allows for separation in theory, the paper does not explicitly address that even with multiple channels, if two classes have samples that are shifts of each other, they will still project to the same circulant covariance structure. The claim that this 'naturally' leads to convolutions is strong, but the actual discriminative power of the resulting lifted subspaces depends heavily on the initial choice of filters $k_c$, which the authors suggest can be 'random'. Random filters may not ensure that the $n$-dimensional subspaces of different classes in the $nC$-dimensional space are sufficiently incoherent.

comments.title: Dimensionality mismatch in 'lifting' operation
comments.quote: This operation lifts each original feature vector $z$ channels of a feature $\in \mathbb{R}^n$ to a $C$-channel feature, $\bar{z}$ as a column vectordenoted $\bar{z}_{vec} = [.] [(\bar{z}_{z} [1]) \in, \dots, \mathbb{R}^{nC} z$ , the associated circulant version$[ C] ] [*] \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times n}$.
comments.explanation: The text describes lifting a vector $z \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to a $C$-channel representation. It defines $\bar{z}_{vec}$ as a column vector in $\mathbb{R}^{nC}$, which is standard for stacked channels. however, it then describes the 'associated circulant version' as being in $\mathbb{R}^{C \times n}$. If the intention is to represent the shifted versions of a multi-channel signal, a signal with $C$ channels and length $n$ under circulant shifts should be represented by a matrix in $\mathbb{R}^{nC \times n}$ (where each column is a shifted version of the stacked $nC$ vector). A $C \times n$ matrix is insufficient to hold the dimensionality of $n$ shifted versions of $C$ channels unless the authors are implying a lossy projection or a very specific spectral representation not yet defined. This notation creates significant ambiguity regarding how the covariance matrix $\Sigma$ is constructed for multi-channel data.

comments.title: Subspace rank collapse in shift-invariant representations
comments.quote: In general, the set of all circular permutations of a vector $z$ give a full-rank matrix. That is, the $n$ “augmented” features associated with each sample (hence each class) typically already span the entire space $\mathbb{R}^n$. The $\text{MCR}^2$ objective (1) will not be able to distinguish classes as different subspaces.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that the orbit of a generic signal under a shift group spans $\mathbb{R}^n$, which makes subspace-based discrimination impossible. They propose 'lifting' to a higher dimension $nC$ via $C$ filters to solve this. However, mathematically, lifting a signal via linear convolutions does not increase the rank of the circulant matrix orbit in a way that creates separability if the underlying group action is still the same shift. If $z$ is a vector and $\bar{z}$ consists of $z \circledast k_c$, the columns of the multi-channel circulant matrix are still tied to the same $n$ degrees of freedom. Unless the filters $k_c$ are non-linear or the features are made sparse (as hinted in the header but not elaborated in the text), the $n$ shifted versions of the lifted signal will still span an $n$-dimensional subspace of the $nC$ space. While $n < nC$, the $k$ classes will still likely overlap significantly because their orbits are all generated by the same shift operator on the same base space. The claim that lifting alone solves the 'full-rank' problem is mathematically underspecified.

comments.title: Unclear notation for multi-channel covariance
comments.quote: If we stack the multiple circ( $\bar{z}$ ) and its data covariance matrix, denoted as $\mathbf{\Sigma}[\bar{]}$, for all its shifted versions are given as:
comments.explanation: The OCR/text cuts off before the formula for $\Sigma$, but the preceding text suggests $\Sigma$ is the covariance of the 'shifted versions'. If $\bar{z}$ is the multi-channel feature, the covariance $\Sigma$ should be an $nC \times nC$ matrix. The text implies the circulant property is preserved, but for multi-channel data, the covariance is 'block-circulant', not simply circulant. This is a crucial distinction for the claim that linear operators 'naturally become multi-channel convolutions.' Standard convolutions correspond to circulant matrices; multi-channel convolutions correspond to block-circulant matrices with circulant blocks. The prose glosses over this geometric complexity, which is vital for the 'white box' derivation of a standard CNN layer.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper proposes an ambitious and theoretically grounded derivation of deep convolutional networks from the first principles of rate reduction and shift-invariant classification, positioning itself as a “white box” alternative to conventional deep learning frameworks that rely on black-box backpropagation. The core idea—using gradient ascent on the Maximal Coding Rate Reduction (MCR²) objective to construct network layers iteratively in a forward manner—is conceptually novel and offers a promising direction toward interpretable deep architectures with well-defined statistical and geometric meanings. The clarity of exposition is generally strong: the motivation is compelling, the technical setup is introduced with care, and the connections to prior work in representation learning and information theory are appropriately acknowledged. However, the most significant issue lies in the incompleteness of the technical narrative—specifically, the abrupt truncation of key equations and explanations toward the end of the provided text (e.g., the MCR² objective is cut off mid-equation, and the spectral domain discussion is left unfinished), which severely limits the ability to evaluate the soundness and rigor of the proposed derivation. This fragmentation undermines confidence in the paper's central claims, particularly regarding how precisely the gradient updates translate into convolutional structures or how invariance constraints mechanically induce multi-channel convolutions in the spectral domain. Moreover, while the claim of achieving good performance without backpropagation is tantalizing, the preliminary nature of the experiments and lack of concrete benchmarks make it difficult to assess practical efficacy. To strengthen impact, the authors must ensure full presentation of their mathematical framework and provide more detailed empirical validation that directly compares representational quality and efficiency against standard architectures. Nonetheless, the high-level vision—deriving deep networks not as engineered artifacts but as outcomes of principled optimization—is both timely and significant, and with more complete technical execution, this could become a foundational contribution to the theoretical understanding of deep learning.

comments.title: Unclear normalization constraint in gradient-guided update
comments.explanation: The use of the proportionality symbol '∝' in the update rule is ambiguous when combined with the constraint that **z**_{ℓ+1} ∈ S^{n−1}. Typically, such updates are followed by explicit normalization, e.g., P_{S^{n−1}}(**z**_ℓ + η·g(**z**_ℓ, **θ**_ℓ)). The symbol '∝' suggests affine transformation followed by rescaling to unit norm, which aligns with the goal of keeping features on the sphere. However, without stating the exact normalization procedure (e.g., l²-normalization), there is ambiguity: Is it direction only? Is it post-hoc normalization? Or is ∝ just informal for 'is set to'? Later text refers to P_{S^{n−1}}, so the authors likely mean a projection, but here in Equation (7), the notation is underspecified. Given that subsequent derivations depend on the geometry of the sphere, especially in computing gradients and interpreting projections, the lack of clarity about the exact update mechanism risks misinterpretation. This is particularly relevant since the gradient ascent occurs on a constrained manifold. While context partially resolves this (via later use of P_{S^{n−1}}), the abrupt switch from '∝' to operator notation is jarring and underspecified at first introduction.

comments.explanation:

comments.quote:
By inspecting the structure of the gradient (6), it suggests that a natural candidate for the increment transform _g_ ( _**z** ℓ,_ _**θ** ℓ_ ) is of the form:

_j_ =1 where _**π**[j]_ ( _**z** ℓ_ ) _∈_ [0 _,_ 1] indicates the probability of _**z** ℓ_ belonging to the _j_ -th class.[3]
comments.explanation: The text claims that inspecting the gradient (which is not fully shown) suggests g(**z**_ℓ, **θ**_ℓ) should take a particular linear-combination form involving **E**_ℓ and **C**_{ℓj}. However, no derivation or citation is given for why this specific functional form emerges from ∂∆R/∂**Z**. From the MCR² framework, we expect the gradient to involve differences between global and class-conditional covariance matrices, possibly scaled by inverses or pseudo-inverses. The proposed g includes terms like (**E**_ℓ − ∑ γ_j **C**_{ℓj}^T **C**_{ℓj}) **z**_ℓ and a second term weighted by π_j(**z**_ℓ), but the connection between this structure and the derivative of ∆R(**Z**) is not justified here. Given that this choice directly defines the architecture of each ReduNet layer, omitting justification makes the construction appear post hoc rather than derived. The authors correctly emphasize derivation from first principles, but this step breaks that thread unless completed elsewhere.

comments.title: Incorrect inclusion of γ_j and λ in layer-wise parameters θ_ℓ
comments.quote: _**θ** ℓ_ collecting all the layer-wise parameters including _**E** ℓ,_ _**C** ℓ[j][, γ][j]_[and] _[ λ]_[, and with features at each layer always “normalized” onto a sphere]
comments.explanation: The passage states that \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_\ell\) includes \(\mathbf{E}_\ell, \mathbf{C}_{\ell j}, \gamma_j,\) and \(\lambda\. However, according to the initial key definitions, \(\gamma_j = \text{tr}(\boldsymbol{\Pi}_{j}) / m\) is a data-dependent scalar defined from class membership counts, not a learnable or layer-specific parameter. It is computed once from training labels and remains constant across layers—thus it should not be part of \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_\ell\), which collects layer-\(\ell\)-specific transform parameters. Including \(\gamma_j\) in \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_\ell\) incorrectly suggests it is updated or constructed per layer. Furthermore, \(\lambda\) is a scalar hyperparameter (temperature in softmax), likely fixed across layers, not a layer-wise parameter. While \(\mathbf{E}_\ell\) and \(\mathbf{C}_{\ell j}\) are correctly layer-specific, listing \(\gamma_j\) and \(\lambda\) as components of \(\boldsymbol{\theta}_\ell\) misrepresents their role. This could mislead a reader attempting to implement the model about what is recomputed per layer. The error persists even under plausible OCR correction, indicating a conceptual overstatement in parameterization.

comments.title: Ambiguity in construction and dimensionality of multi-channel circulant features
comments.quote: where circ( _**z**_ [ _c_ ]) _∈_ R _[n][×][n]_ with _c ∈_ [ _C_ ] is the circulant version of the _c_ -th channel of the feature _**z**_ ¯. Then the columns of circ(¯ _**z**_ ) will only span at most an _n_ -dimensional proper subspace in R _[nC]_ .
comments.explanation: There is a dimensional inconsistency: each circ(**z**[c]) ∈ ℝ^{n×n}, and stacking C such matrices would not yield a matrix in ℝ^{nC×nC} unless explicitly structured as a block-circulant matrix. The claim that columns of circ(¯ **z**) span a subspace in ℝ^{nC} implies that circ(¯ **z**) ∈ ℝ^{nC×T}, but how the per-channel circulants are combined is not specified. The paper likely intends a block-circulant-with-circulant-blocks (BCCB) structure or vectorization of the multi-channel feature, but this transition is underspecified. The construction is crucial for justifying convolutional structure and spectral efficiency, yet it lacks clarity on stacking mechanism and resulting dimensionality. OCR issues compound the problem, but the core issue is inadequate description of how multi-channel circulant matrices are formed and how their covariance inherits block-diagonal structure in the Fourier domain.

comments.title: Ambiguity in sparsity thresholding operator τ(·) with conflicting criteria
comments.quote: Hence we may take an entry-wise _sparsity-promoting nonlinear thresholding_ , say _**τ**_ ( _·_ ), on the filter outputs by setting small (say absolute value below _ϵ_ ) or negative responses to be zero:[8]
comments.explanation: The description of the sparsifying operator \tau(\cdot) is ambiguous. The text says to zero values if they are 'small (|x| < ϵ)' OR 'negative'. This leads to confusion: should a large negative value (e.g., -1.0) be zeroed solely due to sign, even if it carries strong signal? The combination of magnitude and sign thresholds creates an asymmetric, poorly defined nonlinearity. While footnote [8] says \tau can be soft thresholding or ReLU, the main text conflates two distinct mechanisms. This underspecification affects reproducibility and interpretation, especially since \tau plays a key role in separating classes on distinct submanifolds. A clear, unambiguous definition (e.g., ReLU, soft thresholding) should be given in the main text, not left to disambiguation via footnote.

comments.title: Mismatch in experimental step size η between shift-invariance regimes
comments.quote: set iteration L = 3 _,_ 500, precision ϵ = 0 _._ 1, stepsize η = 0 _._ 5
comments.explanation: The passage states η = 0.5 for the MNIST rotational invariance experiment, which operates via polar coordinate transformation (effectively testing shift invariance in angular domain). However, earlier settings specify η = 0.1 for shift-invariance tasks and η = 0.5 only for synthetic data. Using η = 0.5 here contradicts the principled regime and may not be justified for a shift-invariance-focused setup. While step size could be dataset-dependent, the paper emphasizes fixed, forward-constructed parameters without tuning, so inconsistency across similar settings undermines methodological coherence. This raises questions about parameter generalization and experimental rigor.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about subspace orthogonality across more classes than trained

comments.explanation: The paper claims that class representations converge to mutually orthogonal subspaces on the test set. However, training is limited to digits '0' and '1'. The claim uses plural 'classes', implying broader orthogonality across more than two. If the test set includes all 10 digits, this suggests generalization to 8 unseen classes, which is not substantiated by the training objective (MCR² for 2 classes). Either the experiment uses more training classes (not stated), or the conclusion overreaches. This affects the validity of claims supported by Figure 10 and undermines the demonstrated scope of separability.

comments.title: Missing specification of group orbit augmentation in polar angle shift experiment
comments.quote: we expect to learn f(·,θ) such that {f(x(p)◦g,θ)}g∈G lie in the same subspace, where g is the shift transformation in polar angle
comments.explanation: The derivation of convolutional structure relies on data augmentation with the full group orbit G (here, cyclic shifts in polar angle). However, the text does not state whether the training data matrix X(p) includes all Γ shifted versions of each sample. Without such augmentation, the circulant structure of the data covariance is not guaranteed, breaking the theoretical justification for convolutional ReduNet layers. This crucial methodological detail is omitted, weakening the link between theory and experiment in the polar coordinate case.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated spectral complexity claim due to nonlinear thresholding
comments.quote: the overall complexity would be _O_ ( _nC_[3] ) instead of _O_ (( _nC_ )[3] ) for inverting a generic _nC × nC_ matrix.[11]
comments.explanation: The claimed complexity reduction to O(nC^3) depends on the data covariance being block-circulant and diagonalizable via DFT into n independent C×C blocks. However, this structure is disrupted by nonlinear operations like the thresholding operator τ(·), unless it is shift-equivariant (e.g., ReLU). The paper does not verify that τ preserves circulant structure. While plausible under certain assumptions, the efficiency claim lacks support given the nonlinearity. This affects the scalability argument and should be qualified or justified.

comments.quote: For each radius ri, i ∈ [C], we can sample Γ pixels with respect to each angle γl = l · (2π/Γ)
comments.explanation: Mapping discrete Cartesian pixels (e.g., 28×28) to a polar grid requires interpolation (e.g., bilinear, nearest-neighbor), but the method is not specified. Poor interpolation may break the exact cyclic shift structure assumed in the theory, undermining the circulant data model and theoretical justification. Since the method relies on exact group action for derivation, omitting this detail weakens reproducibility and validity of invariance claims.
